claimText: The IUCN Green Status Assessment categorizes the lion as 'Largely depleted'.
evidenceExcerpt: The First Green Status Assessment for the Lion categorizes its status as 'Largely depleted'.
examUse: Statement-based question on IUCN Green Status of species.

claimText: The Asiatic Lion is classified as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List, listed in Schedule I of WPA 1972, and Appendix I of CITES.
evidenceExcerpt: The Asiatic Lion is listed as Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List, Schedule I of WPA 1972, and Appendix I of CITES.
examUse: Statement-based question on the conservation status of the Asiatic Lion.

claimText: Asiatic lions possess a distinctive longitudinal skin fold along their belly, which is typically absent in African lions.
evidenceExcerpt: Asiatic lions have a distinctive longitudinal skin fold along the belly, which is usually absent in African lions.
examUse: Comparison question distinguishing Asiatic and African lions.
distractorHooks: African lions have a prominent belly skin fold
Neither lion species has a belly skin fold

claimText: Male Asiatic lions have sparser and shorter manes compared to African lions, making their ears almost always visible.
evidenceExcerpt: Male Asiatic lions have sparser and shorter manes compared to African lions, making their ears almost always visible.
examUse: Statement-based comparison question on physical characteristics of lions.
distractorHooks: Asiatic lions have thicker and longer manes than African lions
African lions have visible ears due to shorter manes
